attributes.stem:
A 4-month-old boy presents to the General Practice clinic with his parents for a routine health check. He is growing well and has no significant medical history. He does not take any regular medications. His immunisations are up to date. His parents mention they have started introducing rice cereal in the past week, which he has tolerated well. They are anxious about introducing other foods as the boy's older sibling developed egg allergy at one-years-old. They ask for advice about introducing egg into their son's diet.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. His weight and length are tracking along the 50th percentile. His skin is clear with no evidence of eczema or other rashes.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate recommendation regarding egg introduction for this infant?
options: Delay egg introduction until 12 months of age
Avoid egg completely until review by an allergist
Perform skin prick testing before introducing egg
Start with egg-containing baked goods only
attributes.explanation:
This case addresses the current evidence-based approach to introducing potential allergenic foods to infants. The infant in this scenario is 4 months old, tolerating solid foods (rice cereal), has no personal history of atopy (such as eczema), but does have a family history of egg allergy in a sibling.

Introduce well-cooked egg now is the most appropriate recommendation based on current evidence. The Australasian Society of Clinical Immunology and Allergy (ASCIA) recommends introduction of solids, including potentially allergenic foods like egg, between 4-6 months of age for all infants (NHMRC Level II evidence). Research shows that infants who start eating egg between 4-6 months have approximately 40% relative risk reduction of developing egg allergy compared to those who start later.

Delay egg introduction until 12 months of age contradicts current evidence and guidelines. Delaying introduction of allergenic foods is no longer recommended and may actually increase the risk of developing food allergies. The old practice of delaying introduction of allergenic foods has been replaced with recommendations for early introduction/consumption to reduce the risk of developing allergy.

Perform skin prick testing before introducing egg is not routinely recommended for infants without a personal history of atopy. While infants with moderate-to-severe eczema or other signs of atopy might benefit from evaluation by an allergist before introducing highly allergenic foods, this infant has no personal risk factors that would warrant pre-emptive allergy testing.

Start with egg-containing baked goods only is an overly cautious approach not supported by current guidelines for infants without personal risk factors. There is no evidence that this stepwise approach is necessary for infants without personal risk factors for food allergy.

Avoid egg completely until review by an allergist is not recommended. Specialist referral is generally reserved for infants with moderate-to-severe eczema or other significant risk factors. A family history alone (sibling with egg allergy) does not necessitate specialist review before introducing egg in an otherwise healthy infant.
learningPoints: Introduction of egg between 4-6 months of age reduces the risk of developing egg allergy by approximately 40% compared to later introduction.
Infants with moderate-severe eczema or family history of atopy should be evaluated by an allergist or appropriately trained GP before egg introduction.
When introducing egg to infants, they should be at least 4 months old and already tolerating some solid foods.
references: RACGP Handbook of Non-Drug Interventions: Egg allergy prevention. First published: February 2017. Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/clinical-resources/clinical-guidelines/handi/a-z/e/egg-allergy-prevention

Ierodiakonou D, Garcia-Larsen V, Logan A, et al. Timing of allergenic food introduction to the infant diet and risk of allergic or autoimmune disease. A systematic review and meta-analysis. JAMA 2016;316(11):1181–92. doi:10.1001/jama.2016.12623.
Infant Feeding and Allergy Prevention - Fast Facts. Australasian Society of Clinical Immunology and Allergy (ASCIA)https://www.allergy.org.au/patients/fast-facts/infant-feeding-and-allergy-prevention
